sections.heading: KEY REGULATORY INSTRUMENTS FOR THE ACTUALIZATION OF CLOUD
sections.title: Cloud computing policy framework for public sector adoption
sections.summary: This section establishes key regulatory instruments for cloud computing implementation in Nigeria. It defines critical terms such as Cloud Computing, Federal Public Institutions, and Cloud Service Providers. The policy framework aims to promote cloud adoption by public institutions and SMEs while ensuring alignment with national digital strategies. The document specifies that cloud computing refers to a model for ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to shared computing resources.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides foundational definitions for cloud computing services and entities involved in public sector adoption, which are critical for understanding data transfer mechanisms when moving government data to cloud environments.

requirements.text: Cloud Computing refers to computing model for ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand and real time network access pool of configurable computing resources
Federal Public Institutions (FPIs) means Ministries, Departments, Extra-Ministerial Departments and Agencies of Government at the Federal level
Cloud Service Providers (CSPs) refer to local and/or international cloud computing service providers rendering service to FPIs and SMEs in Nigeria

sections.source_quote: KEY REGULATORY INSTRUMENTS FOR THE ACTUALIZATION OF CLOUD 23.0
sections.relevance_reason: Definitions of key terms for cloud computing

sections.title: Cloud adoption policy for public sector and SMEs
sections.summary: This section outlines the policy framework for cloud adoption in Nigerian public institutions and SMEs. It specifies that the policy is applicable to Federal Public Institutions, State and Local Government entities, and government-owned corporations. The policy mandates that public-sector entities prioritize cloud-based procurement of ICT solutions to reduce costs and improve service delivery. It also sets targets for 30% cloud adoption by 2024 among public institutions and SMEs providing digital services to government.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates public-sector entities to prioritize cloud-based procurement of ICT solutions, which directly impacts data transfer mechanisms when moving government data to cloud environments.

requirements.text: The Policy is applicable to all Federal Public Institutions, Public Institutions at the State and Local Government levels
The Policy shall also apply to all corporations fully or partially owned by the Federal Government in Nigeria
Upon publication of this policy, Nigerian public-sector entities shall prioritize the procurement of cloud-based information and communication technologies
The policy targets 30% increase in cloud adoption by 2.0 Federal Public Institutions (FPIs) and SMEs that provide digital-enabled services to the government by 2024

sections.source_quote: The Nigeria Cloud Computing Policy is issued pursuant to Section 6 (a) (b) (c) and (i) of the National Information Technology Development Act 2007.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cloud service models and deployment options for public institutions
sections.summary: This section describes cloud computing service models (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS) and deployment models (private, public, hybrid clouds) applicable to Nigerian public institutions. It outlines how these models function, their use cases, and the benefits they offer for digital transformation and resource efficiency in government operations. Public institutions must consider these models when migrating to cloud services to optimize service delivery and cost management.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines cloud service models and deployment options that impact data transfer and storage decisions. Public institutions must select models that align with data sensitivity and security requirements to ensure compliance with Nigerian data protection regulations.

requirements.text: Public institutions must consider cloud service models (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS) when migrating to cloud services
Cloud service providers must not manage underlying infrastructure for SaaS
Cloud service providers must not manage underlying infrastructure for PaaS
Cloud service providers must not manage underlying infrastructure for IaaS
Public institutions must consider deployment models (private, public, hybrid) for cloud services

sections.source_quote: The policy recognizes three basic kinds of cloud computing service offerings or cloud based service models as: i. Software as a Service (SaaS): where the consumer uses the provider's applications running on cloud infrastructure. ii. Platform as a Service (PaaS): PaaS capability is provided to consumer is a pre-installed cloud infrastructure platform... iii. Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS): The consumer can provision processing, storage, networks...
sections.relevance_reason: Cloud models affect data handling but not direct compliance duties

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Data classification and international data flow considerations for cloud services
sections.summary: This section details data classification frameworks for Nigerian public institutions migrating to cloud services, categorizing data into four sensitivity levels: official/public, confidential/routine, secret/sensitive, and classified/national security. It specifies storage requirements for each category, including mandatory residency within Nigeria for sensitive data. The section also addresses cross-border data flows, requiring cloud providers to store data in jurisdictions with equivalent data protection standards to Nigeria.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates data classification and storage requirements that directly impact cross-border data transfers. Public institutions must ensure sensitive data remains within Nigeria while balancing international data flows with equivalent protection standards.

requirements.text: Public institutions must classify data into four sensitivity levels: official/public, confidential/routine, secret/sensitive, classified/national security
Confidential data must reside primarily within Nigerian territorial boundaries
Secret data must reside primarily within Nigerian territorial boundaries
Classified data must reside only within Nigerian territorial boundaries
Cloud providers must store data in jurisdictions with equivalent data protection standards to Nigeria

sections.source_quote: Data will be classified according to the following categories: i. Official, public or non-confidential Data (data of limited sensitivity): This is primarily data that is publicly available and non-sensitive... ii. Confidential, routine government business data (data of moderate sensitivity): This category may include health and financial data about natural persons... iii. Secret, sensitive government and citizen data: This type of data is related to natural and juridical persons... iv. Classified or National security information: This type of data is considered sensitive to national security...
sections.relevance_reason: Data classification, cross-border transfer, residency requirements

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cloud service contracts for government entities require minimum security and data handling terms
sections.summary: This section mandates that NITDA develops a regulatory framework for cloud contracts between public institutions and cloud service providers (CSPs). The framework ensures government entities receive equivalent rights to private customers and includes minimum contractual terms such as CSP due diligence compliance, service descriptions, contract duration, payment terms, SLAs, data handling procedures, data retrieval rights upon termination, liability limitations, and security certifications. These requirements apply to all Nigerian government entities using cloud services.
sections.dtia_summary: Government entities must include specific contractual terms in cloud agreements to ensure data security, data portability, and liability protections when transferring data to CSPs across borders. This section directly impacts cross-border data transfers by requiring contractual safeguards for government data.

requirements.text: CSP must adhere to due diligence process and public procurement guidelines
Contract must specify service descriptions
Contract must define duration unless unlimited
Contract must include payment terms and termination clauses
Contract must detail Service Level Agreements (SLAs)
Contract must specify data handling procedures including processing, destruction, and restoration
CSP must provide customer care services depending on service offering
Government entities must retain right to retrieve data upon contract termination
CSP must limit liability for data loss, damage, service unavailability, or breaches
Contract must specify cloud security and privacy standards

sections.source_quote: NITDA will work on the development of a regulatory framework for the execution of cloud computing contracts between Public Institutions and Cloud Service Providers (CSP). This regulatory framework shall ensure that government entities using the cloud as cloud customers enjoy at least the same rights as those enjoyed by individual customers, enterprises and other cloud customers.
sections.relevance_reason: Cloud security, data handling, government contracts

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Cloud governance framework for public sector with four-tiered oversight
sections.summary: This section establishes a four-tiered cloud governance framework for Nigerian public sector entities, with responsibilities distributed across NITDA, Bureau of Public Procurement, ONSA, and other bodies. It requires NITDA to conduct cloud readiness assessments, coordinate cloud adoption, monitor security standards, and develop procurement regulations for cloud services. The framework mandates specific operational categories at each governance level to ensure accountability, risk management, and alignment with national objectives.
sections.dtia_summary: Public sector entities must implement a structured governance framework with defined roles to manage cloud services, ensuring data security, compliance, and interoperability across different cloud tiers. This section is critical for DTIAs as it specifies how data is governed across borders through contractual and operational safeguards.

requirements.text: Bureau of Public Procurement must develop government-wide cloud procurement regulations
NITDA must liaise with BPP to accommodate cloud procurement models
NITDA must conduct cloud readiness assessments
ONSA and NITDA must monitor operational security issues related to cloud
NITDA must drive government-wide cloud adoption and identify next-generation technologies
NITDA must coordinate cloud-related activities across governance bodies
NITDA must monitor and prioritize cloud standards from NIST, ISO, and other bodies

sections.source_quote: The Bureau of Public Procurement shall develop and operationalize government-wide procurement regulation for Cloud services in consultation with NITDA and other relevant agencies of government
sections.relevance_reason: Cloud governance for public sector data security
